title: The 25 Best Movies of the Century: No. 16 - ‘Oppenheimer’
format: Sean Fennessey and Amanda Dobbins defend Oppenheimer at No. 16 on their 25-best-of-the-century list, walking Christopher Nolan's whole filmography to argue why this one and not The Dark Knight.

films.title: Oppenheimer
films.note: The most recent film on the list and, for the hosts, the culmination of Nolan's career — a billion-dollar, R-rated, three-hour talking drama that won seven Oscars off thirteen nominations. They single out the wordless shot of the bomb leaving Los Alamos in trucks, Cillian Murphy's performance, and Jennifer Lame's editing and Ludwig Göransson's score, both Oscar winners. Amanda's misgivings sit with the third-hour Strauss plotline; both agree the Trinity test is among the best sequences Nolan has filmed. It replaced a 2022 pick Amanda won't yet name.

films.title: Memento
films.note: Sean's dawning-moment Nolan and a great Guy Pearce performance; picking it over Inception or Interstellar would have felt like a troll.

films.title: Insomnia
films.note: The remake Nolan didn't write — good performances, but a rote cat-and-mouse serial-killer thriller. Never in real consideration.

films.title: Batman Begins
films.note: Sets a new template for superhero storytelling but plays as table-setting for the sequel; they're not building a Hall of Fame that rewards the one that starts it.

films.title: The Prestige
films.note: A perennial favorite of the boys-at-home; both call it very good, more than above-average, but short of the pick.

films.title: The Dark Knight
films.note: Was on the original list as recently as a month before recording, as the superhero representative, and Sean once argued it won. He took it off: Heath Ledger's Joker is extraordinary, but the air goes out once he leaves and the Two-Face turn goes goofy. No superhero movie made the final 25.

films.title: Inception
films.note: Amanda's would-be No. 1 Nolan and the signature crowd-pleaser — the hallway fight is like nothing Sean had seen — even if it doesn't survive close examination.

films.title: The Dark Knight Rises
films.note: On nobody's list. Fun and very silly; Amanda finds it easier to enjoy precisely because no one calls it the greatest movie ever made.

films.title: Interstellar
films.note: The pick for the generation beneath them. Sean admires the ambition but bounces off the 'love is the answer' ending grafted onto an otherwise sophisticated film.

films.title: Dunkirk
films.note: The one they most strongly considered. Sean loves the triple-timeline structure and its restraint — grave stakes conveyed through the film, not the dialogue — but it doesn't hit him like the next two Nolans.

films.title: Tenet
films.note: A ton of fun and, per Amanda, a movie she understands less every time they discuss it. Not thematically deep, just bros, free ports, and the immutability of friendship — and not as good as Oppenheimer.

segments.heading: The legacy question
segments.note: Lawrence, or How Green Was My Valley?

films.title: Lawrence of Arabia
films.note: Sean's optimistic comp: an all-time historical epic people still describe as breathtaking on a big screen sixty years on. It also won Best Picture.

films.title: How Green Was My Valley
films.note: The pessimist's comp: John Ford's arrival-moment Best Picture winner that beat, and is now overshadowed by, Citizen Kane. Amanda leans toward this being Oppenheimer's fate — respected, but not the film that endures.

films.title: Citizen Kane
films.note: The more daring loser that history remembers, and the model for how a Best Picture winner can be eclipsed.

films.title: The Zone of Interest
films.note: Amanda's Citizen Kane in this analogy — thematically linked, harder to rewatch, but the more formally innovative film she expects to matter more over time.

films.title: Amadeus
films.note: Sean's best match — the same grand-drama energy, scope, and bigness.

films.title: Lincoln
films.note: Offered, then needled: is it really taking an 'are we sure?' approach to its great man?

films.title: Steve Jobs
films.note: The realer 'are we sure he's a great person?' comp — plus the Sorkin dialogue Oppenheimer's writing recalls.

films.title: The Brutalist
films.note: A recent great-man epic both hosts rate.

films.title: Interstellar
films.note: Floated again with a shrug — are we sure it's good? Sure.

films.title: Citizen Kane
films.note: The lead recommendation: a great man, maybe not all he was cracked up to be, with second thoughts on his life.
